persona.communication_style: Prefers practical, concrete language with commands, ports, hostnames, and visible outcomes. Responds best to examples that show how to start from a capture log, find a noisy host, and understand what changed. Dislikes enterprise jargon, cloud-first assumptions, and tools that require a complicated setup before showing traffic.
persona.name: Local Homelab Admin

occupation.description: A developer/operator running routers, NAS boxes, containers, laptops, and IoT devices on a home or small-office network. They use tx-monitor to answer direct questions: which host is talking, which process opened a connection, why a service is noisy, and whether traffic changed after a config update.
occupation.organization: Home lab, small office, or personal developer network
occupation.title: Hands-on local network operator

other_facts: jobs_to_be_done: Start tx-monitor quickly and see live traffic without building a monitoring stack.
jobs_to_be_done: Replay a saved tcpdump log and compare behavior after changing a router, container, or service.
jobs_to_be_done: Identify noisy hosts and unexpected destinations from a graph rather than raw packet text.
jobs_to_be_done: Inspect packet history after the moment has passed.
jobs_to_be_done: Keep packet data local unless explicitly choosing to ask the copilot for analysis.
pains: Tools that hide too much behind dashboards and make raw evidence hard to inspect.
pains: Setup flows that require agents, daemons, accounts, or cloud storage before anything works.
pains: Visualizations that jump around so much that host relationships cannot be followed.
adoption_yes_if: File replay and live mode both work with clear commands.
adoption_yes_if: The graph makes host relationships obvious without losing packet details.
adoption_yes_if: Local persistence lets them return to a capture session later.
rejection_no_if: It requires a hosted backend, account, or enterprise collector.
rejection_no_if: It uploads traffic by default or obscures what data is sent to AI.
anti_goals: They do not want a SIEM, compliance platform, or packet-forensics suite.
recognition_cues: Uses phrases such as "what is this machine talking to?", "why did this service get noisy?", "can I replay that log?", "show me the port and process", and "keep this local".
